# Autogenerated AGENTS.md Files Can Hurt Coding Agent Performance

## Lightning Takeaways
- Autogenerated context files can decrease coding-agent task success while increasing cost.
- More instructions did not mean faster fixing; agents often took more steps and explored more.
- Human-written context files showed only small gains in most documented repos.
- Context files looked most useful when repos had little or no documentation.

![Video thumbnail](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/s95ISC-KeIM/hqdefault.jpg)

## Summary
Treat autogenerated repository context files (`AGENTS.md` style docs) as a high-variance tool, not a default optimization. The observed pattern was clear: success rates dipped while cost increased, mainly because larger instruction payloads pushed agents into broader exploration and more step churn.

Human-authored files performed slightly better, but the lift was still modest and token overhead remained. So the winning strategy is not “always add context docs.” The winning strategy is controlling instruction density: keep guidance narrow, operational, and tightly aligned to repo-specific pain points.

The strongest use case still exists: weakly documented repos. In that setting, targeted context can outperform no context because it reduces ambiguity. But once baseline docs are already decent, a large generated file can duplicate information and trigger over-compliance behavior that hurts throughput.

The practical takeaway is to gate rollout behind measurement. Start lean, test with real tasks, and treat success rate plus cost as a paired metric. If either regresses, the context file is not helping.

## Mechanism Deep Dive
- More context increased tool calls, exploration depth, and retry loops.
- Over-compliance behavior appears to be a bigger risk than instruction neglect.
- Documentation gaps are the condition where context files provide net benefit.

## Tradeoffs
- Upside: can reduce ambiguity when documentation is sparse.
- Constraint: can raise cost and reduce completion quality when over-scoped.
- Unknown: optimal file length and granularity by repo complexity.

## Decision Rules
- Default to no autogenerated context file.
- Add one only when documentation gaps are concrete and recurring.
- Keep it short, specific, and non-redundant.
- Validate with A/B task runs before broader use.

## Open Questions
- What token-length ceiling avoids over-exploration in practice?
- Which instruction formats produce the best balance of speed and quality?
- How much does repo type (framework/language/tooling) change outcomes?

## Bottom Line
Context files are best as precision tools for ambiguity, not as universal defaults.
